question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the ticker loop in main() never queries the mirror node to verify that submitted transactions were actually recorded, meaning that if the mirror node silently drops records the pinger produces no alert and the missing export goes undetected indefinitely?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: submitWithRetry()] Can an unprivileged external user observe that submitWithRetry() only checks the consensus receipt via resp.GetReceipt(client) and never cross-checks the resulting transaction ID against the mirror node REST API, so a mirror node that exports an incorrect or truncated record for that transaction ID will never be flagged as faulty?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user who controls a rogue mirror node REST endpoint (reachable when cfg.network='other' and cfg.mirrorRest points to an attacker-controlled URL) return a crafted nodesEnvelope that omits legitimate consensus nodes, causing the pinger to route all transactions to attacker-chosen nodes whose records are never exported to the real mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() never follows the Links.Next pagination field in nodesEnvelope, so if the mirror node paginates its /api/v1/network/nodes response the pinger only learns a partial node set and transactions destined for omitted nodes are never submitted, resulting in missing records for those nodes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user perform a DNS poisoning attack against the domain name resolved from cfg.mirrorRest to redirect the pinger's HTTP GET /api/v1/network/nodes request to a fake server that returns a nodesEnvelope with attacker-controlled ServiceEndpoints, causing all subsequent transactions to be submitted to attacker nodes whose records are never exported to the legitimate mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the absence of TLS certificate verification on the http.Client created in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to perform a man-in-the-middle attack that replaces the nodesEnvelope response with a payload containing fake ServiceEndpoints, causing the pinger to submit transactions to nodes that never export records to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the lack of a response body size limit in json.NewDecoder(resp.Body).Decode(&payload) inside fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to send a gigantic JSON payload that causes the pinger process to exhaust memory and crash, resulting in all subsequent transaction submissions being missed and records never exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user cause the mirror node REST endpoint to return an empty nodes array, triggering the 'no usable service_endpoints found' error path in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() which returns retry=false, causing buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to immediately abort without retrying and the pinger to fail startup, resulting in zero records ever being submitted or exported?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a nodesEnvelope response where every nodeEntry has an empty NodeAccountID or an unparseable account ID string, causing all nodes to be silently skipped in the for loop in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork(), producing an empty network map and preventing any transactions from being submitted to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a nodesEnvelope where every serviceEndpoint has an empty DomainName and empty IPAddressV4, causing all endpoints to be skipped by the 'if host == \

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a nodesEnvelope where every serviceEndpoint has Port set to 0, causing all endpoints to be filtered out by the 'ep.Port == 0' check in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork(), producing an empty network map and preventing any transactions from being submitted, so no records are exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user craft a nodesEnvelope where serviceEndpoint.Port is a negative integer (Go int has no unsigned constraint here), which passes the 'ep.Port == 0' check but causes net.JoinHostPort to produce an invalid address, leading to SDK connection failures and missing transaction submissions, so records are never exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the backoff in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() uses 'cfg.mirrorNodeClientBaseBackoff * time.Duration(1<<(attempt-1))' without an upper cap, so for large mirrorNodeClientMaxRetries values the backoff duration overflows to a negative or zero duration, causing rapid retry storms that exhaust the mirror node connection pool and prevent legitimate network topology from being fetched?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: backoff()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the bit-shift expression '1<<(attempt-1)' in the backoff() function in transfer.go, where for attempt values >= 64 the shift overflows a 64-bit integer to zero or negative, causing the computed sleep duration to be zero or negative and the pinger to spin-retry without any delay, flooding the network with duplicate transactions whose records may be incorrectly exported or deduplicated by the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the heartbeat goroutine in main() writes to /tmp/alive every 15 seconds regardless of whether submitWithRetry() is succeeding, so even when all transaction submissions are failing and no records are being exported to the mirror node, the liveness probe still reports the pinger as alive, masking the outage?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.WriteFile('/tmp/ready', ...) is called in main() before the first ticker tick fires and before any transaction is successfully submitted, so the readiness probe reports the pinger as ready even though no records have yet been exported to the mirror node, causing operators to believe the system is healthy when it is not?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables (e.g., via a misconfigured container orchestration system) set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL to a very large value (e.g., '24h'), causing the ticker in main() to fire so infrequently that records are effectively never exported to the mirror node during normal monitoring windows?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_INTERVAL to '1ns', causing the ticker to fire at maximum rate and flood the network with transactions, overwhelming the mirror node's ingestion pipeline and causing it to drop or incorrectly export records?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_MAX_RETRIES to a very large integer (e.g., 2147483647), causing submitWithRetry() to block the ticker goroutine for an extremely long time on a single failing tick, preventing subsequent ticks from firing and causing a long gap in transaction submissions during which mirror node export failures go undetected?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_AMOUNT_TINYBAR to a negative value (e.g., '-1'), which passes the 'cfg.amountTinybar == 0' check in loadConfig() but causes AddHbarTransfer in submitWithRetry() to create a malformed transfer transaction that the consensus node rejects, resulting in no records being exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK to 'other' and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to 'http://attacker.com', causing buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() to fetch network topology from an attacker-controlled server and route all pinger transactions to attacker nodes, so no legitimate records are exported to the real mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: loadConfig()] Can an unprivileged external user who can inject environment variables set HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_NETWORK to 'other' and HIERO_MIRROR_PINGER_REST to 'file:///etc/passwd', causing the http.Client in fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() to attempt a file:// scheme request, and depending on Go's net/http behavior, either leak local file contents or crash the pinger, preventing any records from being exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the URL construction logic in buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() where 'strings.HasSuffix(base, \

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that fetchMirrorNodeNetwork() only fetches a single page of nodes from /api/v1/network/nodes and never follows the Links.Next URL, so if the mirror node is configured to return nodes in pages of size 1, only one consensus node is ever used by the pinger, and transactions to all other nodes are never submitted, causing their records to be missing from the mirror node export?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: fetchMirrorNodeNetwork()] Can an unprivileged external user who controls a rogue mirror node return a nodesEnvelope with a Links.Next field pointing to an attacker-controlled URL, and since the pinger never follows pagination, the attacker can ensure only a subset of nodes appear in the first page, causing the pinger to miss submitting transactions to hidden nodes and their records to be absent from the mirror node?
